claim: The problem — manual reconciliation of raw material balances across 4 locations every week from paper forms.
quote: Every Monday I sit down and reconcile the raw material balances across all four locations.

claim: Data source — photos of paper write-off forms sent by managers via WhatsApp.
quote: The managers at each location send me photos of the paper write-off forms in WhatsApp.

claim: Specific episode: yesterday's reconciliation took 2 hours 40 minutes.
quote: Yesterday specifically it was two hours and forty minutes, I timed it, because it was driving me absolutely crazy.

claim: Specific episode: the day before yesterday the milk at the Lenina Street location was off by almost a liter.
quote: The day before yesterday that's exactly what happened, at the location on Lenina Street the milk was off by almost a liter for the shift.

claim: The manager's reaction to the discrepancy — couldn't explain it, just shrugged.
quote: I called the manager, she says, maybe the barista spilled it, maybe she didn't log it, basically she just shrugged.

claim: Current workaround — a parallel paper logbook, photographed once a week and kept on the phone.
quote: The managers keep a paper logbook at the location, I photograph it once a week and keep copies in a separate folder on my phone, just in case, if the accountant asks.

claim: The paper logbook is cross-checked against the Excel spreadsheet manually, by eye.
quote: I cross-check it manually, by eye, on that same Monday morning.

claim: The cost of the problem in time — 3-4 hours a week just on reconciling, not counting calls.
quote: Well usually it's about three to four hours a week just on reconciling, that's not counting the calls to managers when the numbers don't add up.

claim: The cost of the problem in money — ~41,000 rubles in unexplained discrepancies documented for May.
quote: For May it came out to about forty-one thousand rubles in unexplained discrepancies across all locations, I showed that number to the accountant, she was horrified herself.

claim: The respondent sees the amount as noticeable — comparable to a location's rent for 1.5 weeks.
quote: That's roughly like the rent for one location for a week and a half, so yes, it's noticeable.

claim: All four location managers are involved and find the forms inconvenient to fill out.
quote: All four managers complain that filling out the forms is inconvenient, especially in a rush, when there's a line.

claim: The accountant is involved and complains about delayed numbers.
quote: The accountant complains that the numbers come from me late, because I don't manage to reconcile them in time myself.

claim: The co-owner partner asks about discrepancies but doesn't participate in solving the problem.
quote: Mostly he just asks, the solution is all on me, he's more focused on marketing.

claim: Attempt 1 — in January a programmer was hired for 15,000 rubles to build a Google spreadsheet with formulas.
quote: The first — in January I hired a programmer I know, he built me a Google spreadsheet with formulas and automatic discrepancy calculation for fifteen thousand rubles.

claim: Attempt 1 abandoned — managers got confused entering data, formulas would break.
quote: Then the managers started getting confused about which cell to enter what in, the formulas would break if you typed text instead of a number.

claim: Attempt 1 finally abandoned by March.
quote: By March I just stopped checking it, junk had piled up in there anyway.

claim: Attempt 2 — in April a paid subscription to an inventory management app.
quote: The second approach — in April I downloaded an inventory management app, I think it was called something like Cleverence, I don't remember exactly.

claim: Attempt 2 — paid about 2000 rubles for a month's subscription.
quote: I paid something like two thousand rubles there for a month's subscription.

claim: Attempt 2 abandoned — subscription not renewed, money wasted.
quote: Then I just gave up on it, didn't renew the subscription, the money was wasted.

claim: FACT confirmed by behavior — paid hiring of a programmer for 15,000 rubles.
quote: The first — in January I hired a programmer I know, he built me a Google spreadsheet with formulas and automatic discrepancy calculation for fifteen thousand rubles.

claim: OPINION/DECLARATION not confirmed by behavior — answer to a hypothetical question, weak signal.
quote: Listen, that's exactly what I need, I would definitely buy it, honestly, without a second thought.

claim: Sign of NAGGING pain trending toward BURNING — the respondent already paid money for an attempted solution.
quote: I paid something like two thousand rubles there for a month's subscription.

claim: Limitation of the pain-strength conclusion — the respondent herself distinguishes the documented (May) figure from the estimated (March-April) one.
quote: Yes, May I actually calculated from the spreadsheet, but March-April is me estimating from memory right now, I could be off in either direction.

claim: Readiness signal — independently searching for a solution in a topical Telegram chat and reviewing competitor websites.
quote: In one chat for restaurant owners on Telegram I asked for advice, they threw me about five service names.

claim: Limitation of the readiness signal — refusal to prepay upfront without a trial period.
quote: Well to prepay sight unseen right now — probably not, I'd try it for a month first, see if it sticks with the managers.

claim: The hypothesis about willingness to pay market price for a ready-made solution — disproved by rejecting the price of 15,000 rubles/month.
quote: I looked at two of them on their websites, one had pricing starting from fifteen thousand a month, I closed the tab right away, that's expensive for us with four locations.

claim: Risk of a false positive — the polite closing remark about the conversation's usefulness has no behavioral backing.
quote: Yes, very interesting, you really hit the nail on the head with your questions, I enjoyed it a lot.

claim: MAIN INSIGHT: the problem is real — the respondent has already spent money and time on two independent attempts at a solution, including a paid and abandoned attempt with an inventory management app.
quote: The second approach — in April I downloaded an inventory management app, I think it was called something like Cleverence, I don't remember exactly.
